app.intro:
[dim]Guides you from training to deploying HayaKoe TTS models.
Follow the steps below in order.[/dim]

[dim]  [accent]① Training[/accent]       Train a TTS model with voice data.
  [accent]② Quality Report[/accent]  Compare and listen to audio from trained checkpoints.
                    Repeat ①↔② until satisfied.
  [accent]③ Benchmark[/accent]      Measure inference speed on CPU/GPU.
  [accent]④ Publish[/accent]        Upload trained speakers to HF / S3 / local
                    so they can be downloaded at runtime.
                    CPU deployment automatically performs ONNX export.[/dim]
app.menu.prompt: What would you like to do?
app.menu.training: Training Pipeline — Data preprocessing + model training
app.menu.report: Quality Report — Compare audio across checkpoints
app.menu.publish: Publish — Deploy speakers to HF / S3 / local for download

[error]train.list / val.list are empty.[/error]
  Please run train/val split in the data preprocessing tool first.
training.config.param.epochs.label: Training epochs
training.config.param.epochs.help:
Sets how many times the model iterates over the entire training data.

▸ Higher: The model trains longer and audio quality may improve.
  However, too many epochs cause the model to memorize the training data,
  making new sentences sound unnatural (overfitting).
▸ Lower: Training finishes faster, but the model may not learn
  enough and audio quality may suffer.

Recommended: 100–500 utterances → 300–500 epochs
             1000+ utterances → 100–200 epochs
training.config.param.batch_size.label: Batch size (per GPU)
training.config.param.batch_size.help:
Sets how many audio samples are grouped together per training step.

▸ Higher: Faster and more stable training,
  but uses more GPU memory (VRAM).
  Insufficient memory causes "CUDA out of memory" errors.
▸ Lower: Can train with less memory, but slower.

Recommended by GPU: RTX 3060 (12GB) → 2–4
                    RTX 3090/4090 (24GB) → 4–8
training.config.param.nproc_per_node.hel:
Sets the number of GPUs used for training.
With multiple GPUs, data is split for parallel training,
scaling speed proportionally (DistributedDataParallel).

▸ Higher: Training speed scales with GPU count.
  Setting this above the actual number of GPUs causes errors.
▸ Keep at 1: Single-GPU training (default).

Note: Do not change this if you only have 1 GPU.
      Use nvidia-smi to check your GPU count.
training.config.param.bf16_run.label: bfloat16 mixed precision
training.config.param.bf16_run.help:
Reduces computation precision from 32-bit to 16-bit for faster training.

▸ Enabled: Training is ~1.5x faster and uses less GPU memory.
  Requires Ampere or newer GPUs (RTX 30xx, 40xx, A100, etc.).
▸ Disabled: Works on all GPUs, but training is slower.

Note: Enabling on RTX 20xx or older GPUs may cause errors
      or degrade quality.
training.config.param.eval_interval.help:
Sets how often to save intermediate training results (checkpoints).
Checkpoints are snapshots of the model during training, allowing you
to pick the best one later.

▸ Frequent saves: Finer comparison possible,
  but uses more disk space (~240MB per checkpoint).
▸ Rare saves: Saves disk space,
  but you may miss the best model.

Recommended: 500 if total steps are low, 2000–5000 if high
training.config.param.log_interval.label: TensorBoard logging interval (steps)
training.config.param.log_interval.help:
Sets how often training metrics (loss, etc.) are logged to TensorBoard.
TensorBoard is a monitoring tool that shows training progress as graphs.

▸ Frequent logging: More detailed view of the training process.
▸ Rare logging: Smaller log files and
  marginally faster training.

The default (200) is sufficient for most cases.
training.config.param.learning_rate.help:
Controls how much the model adjusts at each step.
Similar to the sensitivity of a bicycle's handlebars.

▸ Higher: Learns faster, but may overshoot and
  cause unstable or divergent training.
▸ Lower: More stable, but very slow training that
  may never reach the optimum.

If unsure, use the default (0.0001).
training.config.param.warmup_epochs.help:
Gradually ramps up the learning rate from 0 at the start of training.
Similar to accelerating slowly when starting a car instead of flooring it.

▸ With warmup: Prevents early instability and
  leads to smoother convergence.
▸ Set to 0: Training starts immediately at the configured learning rate.

Recommended: 0–3 epochs.
             If training is unstable early on, try 1–2.
training.config.param.freeze_JP_bert.hel:
Freezes the weights of the text understanding component (BERT)
so it is not updated during training.

▸ Enabled: Preserves BERT's pre-learned Japanese knowledge.
  Effective at preventing overfitting with small datasets
  (~200 utterances or fewer). Also improves training speed and memory usage.
▸ Disabled: BERT is fine-tuned along with the rest, allowing
  more natural pronunciation and intonation when data is sufficient.

Recommended: 200 utterances or fewer → ON
             500+ utterances → OFF
training.config.param.freeze_style.label: Freeze style encoder
training.config.param.freeze_style.help:
Freezes the style encoder weights that express
speaker tone and emotion.

▸ Enabled: Preserves existing style expressiveness.
  Useful when data is limited or you want to keep
  the base model's emotional range.
▸ Disabled: Freely learns the new speaker's style.
  Generally preferred when data is sufficient.

Recommended: Usually OFF.
             Try ON if training results sound monotonous.
training.config.param.freeze_decoder.hel:
Freezes the decoder weights that generate the final audio.

▸ Enabled: The audio generation component stays unchanged;
  only the text interpretation part is trained.
  Faster training, but limited quality improvement.
▸ Disabled: The decoder is also trained,
  producing audio better suited to the speaker.

Recommended: Usually OFF.
             No need to enable unless you have a specific reason.
report.menu.no_exports:
[warning]No datasets with inference models (.safetensors) found.
Please run training first.[/warning]
report.menu.custom_text_hint: [dim]Enter text one line at a time. Submit an empty line to finish.[/dim]
report.html.title: HayaKoe Quality Report — {name}
report.html.heading: HayaKoe Quality Report
report.html.subtitle: {name} — {timestamp} — {ckpt_count} checkpoints × {text_count} texts
report.html.chart.generator_loss_desc: Overall generator loss. Lower means it fools the discriminator better
report.html.chart.mel_loss_desc: L1 distance between generated and target mel-spectrograms. Measures timbral accuracy
report.html.chart.discriminator_loss_des: Overall discriminator loss. Ability to tell real from generated audio
report.html.chart.kl_loss_desc: KL divergence between posterior and prior distributions. Latent alignment metric
report.html.chart.duration_loss_desc: Phoneme duration prediction error. Speech rhythm and pacing
report.html.chart.fm_loss_desc: Discriminator intermediate feature matching error. Training stabilization term
benchmark.menu.intro:
[accent]Benchmark[/accent] [dim]— HayaKoe inference performance measurement[/dim]

  [dim]Measures the time to convert text to speech.
  Results are shown as a speed multiplier. For example, 10.0x means
  1 second of audio can be generated in 0.1 seconds.
  Above 1.0x is faster than real-time; higher is better.

  · CPU — Uses ONNX Runtime (no GPU required, for server/local deployment)
  · GPU — PyTorch CUDA + torch.compile auto-enabled[/dim]
benchmark.menu.cuda_check_hint: [dim]Check your CUDA driver and PyTorch CUDA build.[/dim]
benchmark.runner.speed_explanation:
[dim]Speed = audio length ÷ inference time (higher is faster)
  e.g. 10.0x → 1 second of audio generated in 0.1 seconds[/dim]
benchmark.html.title: HayaKoe Benchmark Report
benchmark.html.heading: HayaKoe Benchmark Report
benchmark.html.how_to_read_content:
Speed is the audio generation rate relative to real-time.
Audio length divided by inference time — higher is faster.
e.g. 10.0x = 10 seconds of audio generated in 1 second

Backend is the inference engine used.
· ONNX (CPU) — No GPU needed. Suitable for server/local deployment.
· PyTorch (CUDA) — Uses NVIDIA GPU. Suitable for high-speed inference.

10x+ (very fast) 1x–10x (faster than real-time) Below 1x (slower than real-time)
benchmark.html.detailed_note: Average of 5 inference runs each for short/medium/long texts. (Excludes 2 warmup runs)
publish.menu.intro_panel:
[accent]Publish Speaker[/accent] [dim]— HF / S3 / Local[/dim]

[dim]Upload trained speakers to a HuggingFace private repo, S3 bucket,
or local directory so they can be downloaded at runtime.
Afterward, use in your runtime:
  TTS(...).load(name, source="hf://...").prepare()

Choosing CPU deployment will automatically perform ONNX export if needed.[/dim]
publish.menu.no_trained_speakers: [dim]No trained speakers found — you can select a folder manually to upload.[/dim]
publish.backend.cpu: CPU (ONNX) — For servers/local without GPU, BERT Q8 + ONNX Runtime
publish.backend.gpu: GPU (PyTorch) — torch.compile + CUDA Graph, minimum latency
publish.backend.both: CPU + GPU (recommended) — Deploy for both environments
publish.checkpoint.select_prompt: Select checkpoint (the best model from the quality report)
publish.destination.prompt: Where should it be downloadable from?
publish.hf.address_hint:
[dim]HF repo address — all of the following formats are accepted:
    lemondouble/hayakoe-voices
    hf://lemondouble/hayakoe-voices
    hf://lemondouble/hayakoe-voices@main
    https://huggingface.co/lemondouble/hayakoe-voices
    https://huggingface.co/lemondouble/hayakoe-voices/tree/dev[/dim]
publish.summary.title: [accent]Deploy summary[/accent]
publish.summary.speaker: Speaker:      [value]{name}[/value]
publish.overwrite.existing_dirs: [warning]Speaker directories already exist at the destination:[/warning]
publish.overwrite.explanation:
[dim]Uploading as-is may leave old artifacts with different filenames mixed in.
  Proceeding will completely remove the directories above and replace them
  with new files — other speakers and root files (README, etc.) are untouched.[/dim]
publish.readme.no_readme:
[dim]No README.md found at the destination root. A README.md describing the
  data storage format (ko/en/ja/zh) can be auto-generated and uploaded.
  (Runtime usage will list {count} speaker(s): {speakers})[/dim]
publish.readme.confirm_create: Auto-generate README.md and include in deployment?
publish.readme.exists_warning:
[warning]A README.md already exists at the destination — it will not be
  overwritten automatically. Review the diff below and approve to update.
  Declining will keep the existing README.md as-is.[/warning]
publish.readme.confirm_update: Update README.md with the new template? (Decline to keep remote README)
publish.readme.skipped: [dim]Skipping README.md update — remote README remains unchanged.[/dim]
publish.verify.title: [accent]Verification[/accent] [dim]— Downloading from the upload destination and synthesizing "{text}".[/dim]
publish.verify.cuda_skip: [dim]CUDA is not available; skipping GPU (PyTorch) verification.[/dim]
publish.credentials.hf_hint: [dim]Generate a token with write permissions at https://huggingface.co/settings/tokens.[/dim]
publish.credentials.aws_hint:
[dim]If using an S3-compatible endpoint like MinIO / R2 / Tigris,

publish.credentials.endpoint_prompt: Endpoint URL (MinIO/R2/etc., press Enter for standard AWS)
